_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 1
questions.prompt: Which declaration lets a class promise the methods in Payable?
choices: class Invoice implements Payable
class Invoice imports Payable
class Invoice includes Payable
class Invoice instanceOf Payable
questions.explanation: The `implements` keyword connects a class to an interface contract. The class must then provide the required abstract methods.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 2
choices: It asks the compiler to verify a matching parent method
It makes the method private
It permits two return types
It creates the superclass
questions.explanation: The annotation catches misspelled names and mismatched parameters at compile time. It does not change access or create inheritance by itself.

questions.title: Trace Inheritance & Interfaces
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Both classes satisfy Payable, so one list can hold both. The loop calls the version belonging to the actual object. The resulting output is '25.0\n10.0'.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Use an interface to state a capability.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use an interface to state a capability. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices: Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A method that receives the interface accepts any current or future implementation. Requiring one concrete class closes that door.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Inheritance means is-a'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Use `extends` only when every child truly is a usable version of the parent. If one class merely needs another object's help, keep that helper in a field and use composition.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `extends` only when every child truly is a usable version of the parent. If one class merely needs another object's help, keep that helper in a field and use composition.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Type variables by the contract when callers need flexibility.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Type variables by the contract when callers need flexibility. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Use `@Override` for compiler checking.
questions.explanation: Use `@Override` for compiler checking. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Reserve `extends` for a true is-a relationship.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Reserve `extends` for a true is-a relationship. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Inheritance & Interfaces practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Inheritance & Interfaces?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Prefer composition for simple code reuse.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Prefer composition for simple code reuse. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
